title: Metabolic Enzymes in the Nucleus — Nature Comm. 2026

pages.layout_rationale: Four panels to show the four key findings of this study.

panels.visual_description: Lab setting. Dr. Kenji stands at the whiteboard, looking at a chromatome proteomic analysis result.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji] Look at this! Hundreds of metabolic enzymes in the nucleus across tissues.

source_text_ref.excerpt: Native chromatome proﬁling reveals hundreds of metabolic enzymes in the nucleus across tissues

panels.visual_description: Lab setting. Dr. Kenji stands at the whiteboard, looking at a chromatome proteomic analysis result on a computer screen.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji] We're finding that metabolic enzymes are tissue-speciﬁc, with oxidative phosphorylation proteins notably depleted in lung cancer.

source_text_ref.excerpt: We show that the abundance of metabolic enzymes on chromatin is tissue-speciﬁc, with oxidative phosphorylation proteins notably depleted in lung cancer

panels.visual_description: Lab setting. Dr. Kenji looks surprised, with a thought bubble above their head.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji] But what about the dynamic chromatin association of one-carbon folate enzymes? They're involved in DNA damage and repair processes!

source_text_ref.excerpt: Further, we explore the dynamic chromatin association of onecarbon folate enzymes, demonstrating their involvement in DNA damage and repair processes

panels.visual_description: Lab setting. Dr. Kenji smiles, looking at a chromatome proteomic analysis result on the whiteboard.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji] This study proposes and validates novel non-canonical nuclear roles for several metabolic enzymes.

source_text_ref.excerpt: This study proposes and validate novel non-canonical nuclear roles for several metabolic enzymes, providing new insights into the functional relationship between metabolism and chromatin regulation

pages.layout_rationale: To effectively visualize the multiple examples and mechanisms of metabolic enzymes' nuclear functions.

panels.visual_description: Lab setting. Dr. Kenji stands at a whiteboard, with various diagrams of cells and enzymes on it.
panels.dialogue: [Asst. Rei] The connection between epigenetics and metabolism is complex and has been studied extensively.
panels.character_expression: Dr. Kenji's eyes widen as he points to the diagram.

source_text_ref.excerpt: Classic examples, such as the c-Myc oncogene-BRD4 axis driving the transcription of folate metabolism enzymes8, or mutations in the enzymes Isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 and 2 (IDH1 and IDH2) leading to aberrant genomewide DNA and histone hypermethylation9.

panels.visual_description: A detailed illustration of the nucleus, showing various metabolic enzymes at work.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji] Recent evidence suggests a more direct interaction between epigenetics and metabolism, particularly through nuclear and chromatin translocation of metabolic enzymes.
panels.character_expression: Dr. Kenji's hands gesture emphatically as he speaks.

source_text_ref.excerpt: Metabolic enzymes traditionally associated with cytoplasmic or organelle-specific metabolic reactions have also been shown to carry out distinct functions within the nucleus, phenomena often referred to as nuclear moonlighting functions10–13.

panels.visual_description: A diagram showing the specific example of Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) nuclear localization.
panels.dialogue: [Asst. Rei] For instance, GAPDH nuclear localization is required to transcriptionally control apoptosis-related genes14, but also to facilitate DNA damage repair by stabilizing RAD5115 and promoting the nuclear localization of Nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT)16.
panels.character_expression: Dr. Kenji's eyes narrow in thought as he studies the diagram.

source_text_ref.excerpt: Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) nuclear localization is required to transcriptionally control apoptosis-related genes14, but also to facilitate DNA damage repair by stabilizing RAD5115 and promoting the nuclear localization of Nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT)16.

panels.visual_description: A summary diagram showing the overall connection between epigenetics, metabolism, and the nucleus.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji] Our study provides a unique compendium of chromatin-associated metabolic activities that will serve as the foundation for the global analysis of chromatin and nuclear metabolism.
panels.character_expression: Dr. Kenji smiles, satisfied with his work.

source_text_ref.excerpt: Our study provides a unique compendium of chromatin-associated metabolic activities that will serve as the foundation for the global analysis of chromatin and nuclear metabolism.

pages.layout_rationale: To display the results and discussion sections side-by-side while maintaining a clear narrative flow.

panels.visual_description: Lab setting. Dr. Kenji stands at the whiteboard, facing Asst. Rei, who sits at a nearby bench.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji]: 'We optimized a previously published chromatin enrichment protocol to analyze chromatin-bound proteins across diverse tissue types.'
panels.character_expression: Dr. Kenji's hands gesture emphatically as he writes on the whiteboard.

source_text_ref.excerpt: we optimized a previously published chromatin enrichment protocol62, consisting of sequential cytoplasmic and nuclear lysis followed by sonication and benzonase-mediated chromatin digestion to extract the chromatome

panels.visual_description: Dr. Kenji and Asst. Rei examine a microscope slide with an antibody against mitochondrial Ferredoxin 1 (FDX1). The sample is stained, and FDX1 is visible as a bright spot.
panels.dialogue: [Asst. Rei]: 'The antibody targets proteins that exclusively reside in the mitochondria, ensuring systematic depletion of non-nuclear proteins before nuclear lysis.'
panels.character_expression: Asst. Rei's eyes are narrowed in focus as she examines the microscope slide.

source_text_ref.excerpt: a protein that, according to subcellular fractionation data28, exclusively resides in the mitochondria (Fig. S1A) and that has not been previously identiﬁed as enriched in chromatome-MS experiments conducted by our group26,27

panels.visual_description: Dr. Kenji and Asst. Rei stand in front of a data-independent acquisition mass spectrometry (DIA-MS) machine, with samples displayed on the screen.
panels.dialogue: [Dr. Kenji]: 'We retrieved ~5100 proteins per sample using DIA-MS, most quantiﬁed by multiple peptide-to-spectrum matches.'
panels.character_expression: Dr. Kenji's expression turns thoughtful as he gazes at the data on the screen.

source_text_ref.excerpt: ~5100 proteins per sample, most quantiﬁed by multiple peptide-to-spectrum matches (Fig. 1E, Source Data 2)

panels.visual_description: Dr. Kenji and Asst. Rei sit at a table with papers, looking through the results together.
panels.dialogue: [Asst. Rei]: 'We found that nuclear moonlighting is far more widespread than previously appreciated.'
panels.character_expression: Asst. Rei's eyes light up as she turns to Dr. Kenji with excitement.

source_text_ref.excerpt: recent evidence suggests that nuclear moonlighting is far more widespread than previously appreciated.
